questions.question: Machine A can complete a job in 6 hours. Machine B can complete the same job in 4 hours. If both machines work together, how many hours will it take to complete the job?
questions.explanation: Rate of A = 1/6 job per hour. Rate of B = 1/4 job per hour. Combined rate = 1/6 + 1/4. LCD = 12: 2/12 + 3/12 = 5/12 job per hour. Time = 1 ÷ (5/12) = 12/5 = 2.4 hours = 2 hours 24 minutes.
questions.trap: Students add the times: 6 + 4 = 10 hours (option E). Never add times — add RATES. The combined time is always less than either individual time.

questions.question: Worker A can paint a fence in 8 hours. Worker B can paint the same fence in 8 hours. Working together, how many hours does it take them?
questions.explanation: Rate of A = 1/8 per hour. Rate of B = 1/8 per hour. Combined rate = 1/8 + 1/8 = 2/8 = 1/4 per hour. Time = 1 ÷ (1/4) = 4 hours. Two workers of equal speed always take exactly half the individual time.
questions.trap: Students choose 16 hours (option D), thinking two workers means twice as long. Working together always takes LESS time than either worker alone.

questions.question: Pump A fills a tank in 12 hours. Pump B fills the same tank in 6 hours. Working together, how many hours does it take to fill the tank?
questions.explanation: Rate of A = 1/12 tank per hour. Rate of B = 1/6 = 2/12 tank per hour. Combined rate = 1/12 + 2/12 = 3/12 = 1/4 tank per hour. Time = 1 ÷ (1/4) = 4 hours.
questions.trap: Students add the times: 12 + 6 = 18, then perhaps halve to 9. Must add rates (1/12 + 1/6 = 3/12 = 1/4), not times.

questions.question: Worker B is 3 times as fast as Worker A. If Worker A can complete a project in 12 days, how many days does it take Worker B to complete the same project alone?
questions.explanation: Worker A's rate = 1/12 job per day. Since B is 3 times as fast, B's rate = 3 × (1/12) = 3/12 = 1/4 job per day. B's time = 1 ÷ (1/4) = 4 days.
questions.trap: Students compute 12 × 3 = 36 days (option E), thinking '3 times as fast' means 3 times as long. Opposite: 3 times faster means 1/3 the time, so 12/3 = 4 days.

questions.question: A pipe fills a cistern in 10 hours. A drain can empty the same cistern in 15 hours. If both are open simultaneously on an empty cistern, how many hours will it take to fill the cistern?
questions.explanation: Fill rate = 1/10 cistern per hour. Drain rate = 1/15 cistern per hour. Net rate = 1/10 − 1/15. LCD = 30: 3/30 − 2/30 = 1/30 cistern per hour. Time = 1 ÷ (1/30) = 30 hours.
questions.trap: Students add the rates (1/10 + 1/15 = 5/30 = 1/6) instead of subtracting, getting 6 hours (option B). A drain empties — its rate must be SUBTRACTED from the fill rate.

questions.question: Workers A, B, and C can complete a task in 2 hours, 3 hours, and 6 hours respectively working alone. How long does it take all three working together?
questions.explanation: Rate of A = 1/2, Rate of B = 1/3, Rate of C = 1/6. Combined rate = 1/2 + 1/3 + 1/6. LCD = 6: 3/6 + 2/6 + 1/6 = 6/6 = 1 job per hour. Time = 1 hour exactly.
questions.trap: Students add the three times: 2 + 3 + 6 = 11 hours (option E). Must add RATES not times. The combined rate here equals exactly 1 job/hour — a clean result worth verifying.

questions.question:
Worker A can complete a job in 10 hours. Worker B can complete the same job in 15 hours.

Column A: Time for A and B working together to complete the job
Column B: 6 hours
options: C. The two quantities are equal
D. The relationship cannot be determined from the information given
questions.explanation: Rate of A = 1/10, Rate of B = 1/15. Combined rate = 1/10 + 1/15 = 3/30 + 2/30 = 5/30 = 1/6 per hour. Time = 6 hours. Column A = 6 hours = Column B.
questions.trap: Students estimate 'somewhere between 10 and 15 hours' without computing and guess B. The exact calculation yields exactly 6 hours — always compute rather than estimate.

questions.question: A tap fills a tank at a rate of 1/20 of the tank per minute. How much of the tank is filled after 5 minutes?
questions.explanation: Rate = 1/20 tank per minute. In 5 minutes: (1/20) × 5 = 5/20 = 1/4. The tank is 1/4 full after 5 minutes.
questions.trap: Students report 5/20 and choose B (1/5) thinking 5/20 = 1/5 by canceling 5s incorrectly. 5/20 = 1/4, not 1/5. Divide both by 5: 5÷5 = 1, 20÷5 = 4, giving 1/4.

questions.question: Worker A completes 1/4 of a job per hour. Worker B completes 1/6 of the same job per hour. How many minutes will it take them to complete the entire job working together?
questions.explanation: Combined rate = 1/4 + 1/6. LCD = 12: 3/12 + 2/12 = 5/12 job per hour. Time in hours = 12/5 = 2.4 hours. Convert to minutes: 2.4 × 60 = 144 minutes.
questions.trap: Students compute 12/5 = 2.4 hours and enter 2.4, ignoring the 'minutes' instruction. Always check the unit the question asks for.

questions.question:
Pipe X fills a pool in 8 hours. Pipe Y fills the same pool in 8 hours. Pipe Z drains the pool in 8 hours.

Column A: Hours to fill the pool with X and Y open, Z closed
Column B: Hours to fill the pool with X, Y, and Z all open
options: C. The two quantities are equal
D. The relationship cannot be determined from the information given
questions.explanation: Column A (X and Y only): Fill rate = 1/8 + 1/8 = 2/8 = 1/4. Time = 4 hours. Column B (X, Y, Z): Net rate = 1/8 + 1/8 − 1/8 = 1/8. Time = 8 hours. Column A = 4 hours, Column B = 8 hours. The time value in Column B (8) is greater than Column A (4). Answer: B.
questions.trap: Students confuse 'filling faster' with 'column value is greater'. With X and Y only, the pool fills faster (4h < 8h), but the time value asked about in Column A (4) is LESS than Column B (8). Column B is greater.

questions.question: If 6 workers can complete a construction job in 10 days, how many days would it take 10 workers to complete the same job? (All workers work at the same rate.)
questions.explanation: Total work = 6 workers × 10 days = 60 worker-days. With 10 workers: days = 60 ÷ 10 = 6 days.
questions.trap: Students compute 10 − 6 = 4 days (option A), or try to use 6/10 of 10 days = 6 days without the worker-day concept. The cleanest method: total worker-days = 60, so 60 ÷ 10 workers = 6 days.

questions.question:
Machine A produces 1 widget every 3 minutes. Machine B produces 1 widget every 5 minutes.

Column A: Number of widgets produced by A and B together in 15 minutes
Column B: 8
options: C. The two quantities are equal
D. The relationship cannot be determined from the information given
questions.explanation: Machine A in 15 minutes: 15 ÷ 3 = 5 widgets. Machine B in 15 minutes: 15 ÷ 5 = 3 widgets. Together: 5 + 3 = 8 widgets. Column A = 8 = Column B.
questions.trap: Students mix up A and B's rates (thinking B=3 per 15 min because '3 minutes' is mentioned first), computing 3+5=8 anyway — the total is still 8 but for the wrong reason. Must identify each machine's rate correctly.

questions.question: A job takes 5 days when completed by Worker A alone. Worker A works for 2 days and then stops. What fraction of the job remains?
questions.explanation: Worker A's rate = 1/5 job per day. In 2 days, A completes: 2 × (1/5) = 2/5 of the job. Fraction remaining = 1 − 2/5 = 3/5.
questions.trap: Students choose 2/5 (option A), reporting the fraction completed rather than the fraction remaining. The question asks what REMAINS: 1 − (fraction done) = 1 − 2/5 = 3/5.

questions.question: Two printers together can print a report in 4 hours. Printer A alone takes 12 hours. How many hours does Printer B take alone?
questions.explanation: Combined rate = 1/4 per hour. Rate of A = 1/12 per hour. Rate of B = 1/4 − 1/12. LCD = 12: 3/12 − 1/12 = 2/12 = 1/6 per hour. Time for B = 1 ÷ (1/6) = 6 hours.
questions.trap: Students compute 12 − 4 = 8 hours or 4 − 12 = −8 (subtracting times). Must subtract RATES (1/4 − 1/12 = 1/6), then take the reciprocal to get B's time (6 hours).

questions.question: Worker A's rate is 1/8 of a project per hour. Worker B's rate is twice that of Worker A. What fraction of the project do A and B complete together in 2 hours?
questions.explanation: Rate of A = 1/8 per hour. Rate of B = 2 × (1/8) = 2/8 = 1/4 per hour. Combined rate = 1/8 + 1/4 = 1/8 + 2/8 = 3/8 per hour. In 2 hours: 2 × (3/8) = 6/8 = 3/4.
questions.trap: Students compute B's rate as 1/8 + 2 = 2.125 per hour instead of 2 × (1/8) = 1/4. 'Twice the rate' means multiply the rate value by 2.

questions.question: Worker A can finish a project in 9 hours. Worker B can finish it in 6 hours. Worker A starts alone and works for 3 hours, then Worker B joins. How many more hours after B joins until the project is complete?
questions.explanation: A's rate = 1/9 per hour. In 3 hours, A completes 3/9 = 1/3 of the project. Remaining = 1 − 1/3 = 2/3. B's rate = 1/6 per hour. Combined rate of A and B = 1/9 + 1/6 = 2/18 + 3/18 = 5/18 per hour. Time to finish remaining 2/3: (2/3) ÷ (5/18) = (2/3) × (18/5) = 36/15 = 12/5 = 2.4 hours = 2 hours 24 minutes.
questions.trap: Students forget that A continues working when B joins, computing only B's time to do 2/3: (2/3) ÷ (1/6) = 4 hours (option D). After B joins, both A and B work together at the combined rate 5/18.

questions.question: Pipe A fills a tank in 8 hours. Pipe B fills the same tank in 12 hours. Pipe C drains it in 24 hours. If all three are open simultaneously on an empty tank, how many hours does it take to fill the tank?
questions.explanation: Fill rate of A = 1/8. Fill rate of B = 1/12. Drain rate of C = 1/24. Net rate = 1/8 + 1/12 − 1/24. LCD = 24: 3/24 + 2/24 − 1/24 = 4/24 = 1/6 per hour. Time = 6 hours.
questions.trap: Students add all three rates as fill rates: 1/8 + 1/12 + 1/24 = 3/24 + 2/24 + 1/24 = 6/24 = 1/4, giving 4 hours. The drain pipe empties the tank — its rate must be SUBTRACTED.

questions.question: Worker A and B together can complete a task in 4 hours. Worker A alone takes 6 hours. If Worker B works alone for T hours and completes exactly 1/3 of the task, what is T?
questions.explanation: Combined rate = 1/4. Rate of A = 1/6. Rate of B = 1/4 − 1/6 = 3/12 − 2/12 = 1/12 per hour. For B to complete 1/3: T = (1/3) ÷ (1/12) = (1/3) × 12 = 4 hours.
questions.trap: Students use the combined rate (1/4) as B's rate, getting T = (1/3)/(1/4) = 4/3 hours ≈ 1 h 20 min — not an answer option. Must first find B's individual rate by subtracting A's rate from the combined rate.

questions.question:
Machine X and Machine Y together complete a job in 6 hours. Machine X alone takes 10 hours.

Column A: Time for Machine Y to complete the job alone
Column B: 15 hours
options: C. The two quantities are equal
D. The relationship cannot be determined from the information given
questions.explanation: Rate of X = 1/10. Combined rate = 1/6. Rate of Y = 1/6 − 1/10 = 5/30 − 3/30 = 2/30 = 1/15. Time for Y = 15 hours. Column A = 15 = Column B.
questions.trap: Students subtract times (10 − 6 = 4 hours) or add them (10 + 6 = 16 hours). Must subtract RATES: 1/6 − 1/10 = 1/15, making Y's time 15 hours.

questions.question: A cistern has two inlet pipes. Pipe A fills it in 6 hours, Pipe B fills it in 9 hours. There is a leak that empties the full cistern in 18 hours. If all are open, how long does it take to fill an empty cistern?
questions.trap: Students add all three rates (treating the leak as a fill pipe): 1/6 + 1/9 + 1/18 = 3/18 + 2/18 + 1/18 = 6/18 = 1/3, giving 3 hours. A leak drains — must subtract its rate.

questions.question:
Worker A takes 12 hours and Worker B takes 8 hours to complete a task individually. They work together. Select ALL statements that are true.

A. Together they complete the task in less than 6 hours.
B. Together they complete the task in exactly 4 hours 48 minutes.
C. In one hour working together, they complete 5/24 of the task.
D. Worker B contributes more work per hour than Worker A.
E. Worker A alone is faster than them working together.
options: A. Together they complete the task in less than 6 hours.
B. Together they complete the task in exactly 4 hours 48 minutes.
C. In one hour working together, they complete 5/24 of the task.
D. Worker B contributes more work per hour than Worker A.
E. Worker A alone is faster than them working together.
questions.explanation: Rate of A = 1/12, Rate of B = 1/8. Combined rate = 1/12 + 1/8 = 2/24 + 3/24 = 5/24 per hour. Time = 24/5 = 4.8 hours = 4 hours 48 minutes. A: 4.8 < 6 ✓. B: 4h 48min ✓. C: Combined rate = 5/24 ✓. D: B's rate (1/8 = 3/24) > A's rate (1/12 = 2/24) ✓. E: FALSE — A alone takes 12 hours, which is SLOWER than 4.8 hours together. Working together is always faster than either alone.
questions.trap: Students include option E, thinking 'A is mentioned first so A must be faster.' Working together is always at least as fast as either individual worker alone — never slower.

questions.question: Worker A can complete a job in 15 days. Worker B is twice as fast as Worker A. They start working together, but after 3 days Worker B leaves. How many more days does Worker A need to finish alone?
questions.explanation: Rate of A = 1/15. B is twice as fast: rate of B = 2/15. Combined rate = 1/15 + 2/15 = 3/15 = 1/5 per day. In 3 days together: 3 × (1/5) = 3/5 done. Remaining = 1 − 3/5 = 2/5. A finishes alone: (2/5) ÷ (1/15) = (2/5) × 15 = 6 days.
questions.trap: Students compute the combined work using only A's rate for 3 days: 3 × (1/15) = 1/5 done, leaving 4/5 for A, giving 4/5 ÷ 1/15 = 12 more days. Both A and B worked for 3 days — use the combined rate (1/5 per day).

questions.question: Three friends can clean a house together in 3 hours. The first friend can do it alone in 6 hours and the second can do it alone in 9 hours. How many hours does the third friend take alone?
questions.explanation: Combined rate = 1/3. Rate of friend 1 = 1/6. Rate of friend 2 = 1/9. Rate of friend 3 = 1/3 − 1/6 − 1/9. LCD = 18: 6/18 − 3/18 − 2/18 = 1/18. Time for friend 3 = 18 hours.
questions.trap: Students subtract the known times from the combined time: 3 − 6 − 9 = negative, which is clearly wrong. Must subtract RATES from the combined rate, not times.

questions.question: A tank has an inlet pipe that fills it in T hours and an outlet pipe that drains a full tank in 2T hours. Both are open simultaneously. What fraction of the tank is filled after T hours?
questions.explanation: Fill rate = 1/T per hour. Drain rate = 1/(2T) per hour. Net rate = 1/T − 1/(2T) = 2/(2T) − 1/(2T) = 1/(2T) per hour. Fraction filled in T hours = T × 1/(2T) = 1/2.
questions.trap: Students think the inlet fills in T hours so the tank is full (option E = 1) after T hours. They ignore the drain. With both open, the net rate is 1/(2T), filling only 1/2 the tank in T hours.

questions.question:
n > 0

Column A: Time for Worker A (rate = 1/n per hour) and Worker B (rate = 1/(2n) per hour) to complete a job together
Column B: 2n/3 hours
options: C. The two quantities are equal
D. The relationship cannot be determined from the information given
questions.explanation: Combined rate = 1/n + 1/(2n) = 2/(2n) + 1/(2n) = 3/(2n) per hour. Time = 1 ÷ (3/(2n)) = 2n/3 hours. Column A = 2n/3 = Column B for all n > 0.
questions.trap: Students substitute one value (e.g., n = 6) to get Column A = 4 hours and Column B = 4 hours, mark it equal, but are unsure if this holds generally. The algebra shows 2n/3 always — answer is definitively C.

questions.question: Worker A can complete a report in 10 hours. Worker B takes 40% longer than Worker A. How long does it take them to complete the report working together? (Give the answer in hours and minutes.)
questions.explanation: A's time = 10 hours. B takes 40% longer: B's time = 10 × 1.4 = 14 hours. Rate of A = 1/10. Rate of B = 1/14. Combined = 1/10 + 1/14 = 7/70 + 5/70 = 12/70 = 6/35 per hour. Time = 35/6 ≈ 5.833 hours = 5 hours + 0.833 × 60 ≈ 5 hours 50 minutes.
questions.trap: Students compute B's time as 10 + 0.40 = 10.40 hours (adding 0.40 literally) instead of 10 + 40% of 10 = 10 + 4 = 14 hours. '40% longer' means time = original × 1.40 = 14 hours.

questions.question:
Two pipes together fill a tank in 6 hours. Pipe A alone fills it in 10 hours.

Column A: The fraction of the tank Pipe B alone fills in 5 hours
Column B: 1/3
options: C. The two quantities are equal
D. The relationship cannot be determined from the information given
questions.explanation: Rate of B = 1/6 − 1/10 = 5/30 − 3/30 = 2/30 = 1/15 per hour. In 5 hours, B fills: 5 × (1/15) = 5/15 = 1/3. Column A = 1/3 = Column B.
questions.trap: Students compute B's rate as 1/(6−10) or 1/(10−6) = 1/4 (using time difference instead of rate difference). Must subtract rates: 1/6 − 1/10 = 1/15.

questions.question:
Pipe A fills a tank in 6 hours. Pipe B drains the tank in 10 hours. Both pipes are opened simultaneously on an empty tank. Select ALL statements that are true.

A. The net fill rate is 1/15 per hour.
B. The tank is full after exactly 15 hours.
C. After 6 hours, the tank is 2/5 full.
D. Keeping Pipe B closed would fill the tank faster.
E. With only Pipe A open, the tank fills in less time than with both A and B open.
options: A. The net fill rate is 1/15 per hour.
B. The tank is full after exactly 15 hours.
C. After 6 hours, the tank is 2/5 full.
D. Keeping Pipe B closed would fill the tank faster.
E. With only Pipe A open, the tank fills in less time than with both A and B open.
questions.explanation: Net rate = 1/6 − 1/10 = 5/30 − 3/30 = 2/30 = 1/15 per hour. A: 1/15 ✓. B: Time = 15 hours ✓. C: In 6 hours: 6 × (1/15) = 6/15 = 2/5 ✓. D: Closing B gives rate 1/6 > 1/15, so faster ✓. E: A alone = 6 hours < 15 hours ✓. All five are true.
questions.trap: Students exclude D or E thinking 'opening more pipes always helps.' A drain pipe always slows filling — closing B is strictly better for filling the tank quickly.

questions.question: Worker A starts a job and works for 4 hours, completing 2/5 of the work. Worker B then takes over and finishes the remaining work at twice A's rate. How long does Worker B work?
questions.explanation: A completed 2/5 in 4 hours, so A's rate = (2/5) / 4 = 2/20 = 1/10 per hour. B's rate = 2 × (1/10) = 1/5 per hour. Remaining work = 1 − 2/5 = 3/5. B's time = (3/5) ÷ (1/5) = 3 hours.
questions.trap: Students assume A's time for the full job is 4 hours (rate = 1/4), missing that A only finished 2/5 of the job in 4 hours. Actual rate of A = (fraction done) / time = (2/5) / 4 = 1/10 per hour.

questions.question: Two workers together finish a task in 7.5 hours. One worker alone takes 12 hours. How many hours does the other worker take alone?
questions.explanation: Combined rate = 1/7.5 = 2/15 per hour. Rate of Worker 1 = 1/12. Rate of Worker 2 = 2/15 − 1/12. LCD = 60: 8/60 − 5/60 = 3/60 = 1/20. Time for Worker 2 = 20 hours.
questions.trap: Students compute 7.5 − something or 12 − 7.5 = 4.5 hours. Must convert 7.5 to a fraction (15/2), find combined rate (2/15), then subtract Worker 1's rate (1/12) to find Worker 2's rate (1/20).

questions.question:
Worker A takes 8 hours. Worker B takes 12 hours. They work together, but Worker A leaves exactly 2 hours before the job is done.

Column A: Total time from start to job completion
Column B: 6 hours
options: C. The two quantities are equal
D. The relationship cannot be determined from the information given
questions.explanation: Let T = total time. For (T−2) hours both work; for final 2 hours only B works. Equation: (T−2)(1/8 + 1/12) + 2(1/12) = 1. Combined rate = 5/24. So: 5(T−2)/24 + 2/12 = 1 → 5(T−2)/24 + 4/24 = 1 → 5(T−2) + 4 = 24 → 5T − 10 + 4 = 24 → 5T = 30 → T = 6 hours. Column A = 6 = Column B.
questions.trap: Students set up the equation as T hours of combined work minus A's 2-hour contribution, rather than splitting into two phases. The correct setup: combined for (T−2) hours PLUS B alone for 2 hours.

questions.question: If 4 machines can produce 200 parts in 5 days, how many parts can 6 machines produce in 8 days (at the same rate per machine)?
questions.explanation: Rate per machine per day = 200 ÷ (4 × 5) = 200/20 = 10 parts per machine per day. 6 machines × 8 days × 10 parts/(machine·day) = 480 parts.
questions.trap: Students set up proportion 200/4 = x/6, getting x = 300, ignoring the change in days. Both the change in machines (×6/4) and change in days (×8/5) must be applied: 200 × (6/4) × (8/5) = 480.

questions.question:
Worker A takes 20 hours alone. Worker B takes 30 hours alone. They work together for t hours (0 ≤ t ≤ 12). Select ALL values that could be the fraction of the job REMAINING after t hours.

A. 11/12
B. 3/4
C. 1/2
D. 1/6
E. 0
questions.explanation: Combined rate = 1/20 + 1/30 = 3/60 + 2/60 = 5/60 = 1/12 per hour. Fraction remaining after t hours = 1 − t/12. A: 11/12 → t = 1 h ✓ (0≤1≤12). B: 3/4 → t = 3 h ✓. C: 1/2 → t = 6 h ✓. D: 1/6 → t = 10 h ✓. E: 0 → t = 12 h ✓ (job exactly done). All five are achievable.
questions.trap: Students exclude E=0 thinking the job can't be exactly done within the given constraint, or exclude A=11/12 thinking they must have done a substantial fraction. Any fraction from 0 to 1 inclusive is achievable.

questions.question: A contractor hires 10 workers to finish a building in 20 days. After 5 days, 4 workers leave. How many additional days does it take the remaining workers to finish?
questions.explanation: Total work = 10 × 20 = 200 worker-days. Work done in first 5 days = 10 × 5 = 50 worker-days. Remaining = 200 − 50 = 150 worker-days. Workers remaining = 10 − 4 = 6. Additional days = 150 ÷ 6 = 25 days.
questions.trap: Students compute remaining days as 20 − 5 = 15 days (option A), ignoring that fewer workers remain. The remaining work must be divided by the new (reduced) workforce.

questions.question: Worker A completes 1/3 of a job in 4 hours, then Worker B completes the remaining 2/3 alone. The entire job took 10 hours total. How many hours would Worker B take to complete the full job alone?
questions.explanation: A works for 4 hours and does 1/3. B works for 10 − 4 = 6 hours and does 2/3. B's rate = (2/3) / 6 = 2/18 = 1/9 per hour. B's time for the full job = 9 hours.
questions.trap: Students report B's actual working time (6 hours) as the answer, instead of using B's rate to find how long B would take for the entire job (1 ÷ (1/9) = 9 hours).

questions.question:
Pipe A fills a tank in 12 hours. Pipe B fills it in 18 hours. Pipe C drains it in 36 hours.

Column A: Hours to fill the tank with Pipes A and C open (B closed)
Column B: Hours to fill the tank with Pipes A and B open (C closed)
options: C. The two quantities are equal
D. The relationship cannot be determined from the information given
questions.explanation: Column A (A+C, with drain): Net rate = 1/12 − 1/36 = 3/36 − 1/36 = 2/36 = 1/18. Time = 18 hours. Column B (A+B, no drain): Combined rate = 1/12 + 1/18 = 3/36 + 2/36 = 5/36. Time = 36/5 = 7.2 hours. Column A = 18 hours, Column B = 7.2 hours. Column A > Column B. Answer: A.
questions.trap: Students compute Column B and Column A correctly but then compare 'faster filling' rather than 'column value is greater.' The time value in Column A (18) is larger than Column B (7.2).

questions.question: A water tank is 3/4 full. An inlet pipe fills it at 1/8 tank per hour, and a drain removes water at 1/24 tank per hour. How many hours until the tank is completely full?
questions.trap: Students apply the net rate to the full tank (1 unit) instead of only the remaining 1/4, getting time = 1 ÷ (1/12) = 12 hours. Must use fraction remaining (1/4), not the full tank capacity.

questions.question:
Worker A takes a hours and Worker B takes b hours to complete a job, where b > a > 0.

Column A: Time for A and B to complete the job together
Column B: a/2
options: C. The two quantities are equal
D. The relationship cannot be determined from the information given
questions.explanation: Combined time = ab/(a+b). Compare ab/(a+b) vs a/2. Cross-multiply (all positive): 2ab vs a(a+b) = a²+ab. Subtract ab: ab vs a². Divide by a (a > 0): b vs a. Since b > a, we have b > a, so ab > a², meaning 2ab > a²+ab, meaning ab/(a+b) > a/2. Column A > Column B. Answer: A.
questions.trap: Students think the combined time must be less than a/2 because two workers are faster than one. But a/2 is the time if B were identical to A; since B is slower (b > a), the combined time exceeds a/2.

questions.question: Workers A and B alternate daily (A on day 1, B on day 2, A on day 3, etc.). A can complete the job alone in 10 days; B in 15 days. On which day is the job first completed?
questions.explanation: Rate of A = 1/10, Rate of B = 1/15. Per 2-day cycle: 1/10 + 1/15 = 3/30 + 2/30 = 5/30 = 1/6. After 6 complete cycles (12 days): total work = 6 × (1/6) = 1. The job is exactly completed at the end of day 12. (Day 12 is an even day, so B finishes it.) Answer: Day 12.
questions.trap: Students compute 1 ÷ (average daily rate) = 1 ÷ (5/60) = 12 days but then guess without checking whether the job finishes exactly at a cycle boundary. Here it does — but must always verify.

questions.question: Workers A and B alternate daily (A on odd days, B on even days). A's rate is 1/8 per day; B's rate is 1/12 per day. On which day is the job first completed?
questions.explanation: Per 2-day cycle: 1/8 + 1/12 = 3/24 + 2/24 = 5/24. After 4 full cycles (8 days): work = 4 × 5/24 = 20/24 = 5/6. Remaining = 1/6. Day 9 is A's day (odd): A does 1/8. 1/8 = 3/24 < 4/24 = 1/6, so not done. After day 9: 5/6 + 1/8 = 20/24 + 3/24 = 23/24. Remaining = 1/24. Day 10 is B's day: B does 1/12 = 2/24. Since 2/24 > 1/24, job is completed within day 10. Answer: Day 10.
questions.trap: Students compute 24/5 = 4.8 cycles ≈ 9.6 days and round to day 10 without checking which worker is on that day and whether they can finish the remaining work. Must track remaining work day-by-day after the last complete cycle.

questions.question: Pipe A fills a tank in 6 hours. Pipe B fills it in 9 hours. Pipe C drains it in 4 hours. Pipes A and B are open from the start. After 3 hours, Pipe C is also opened. How many total hours from the start does it take to fill the tank?
questions.explanation: Phase 1 (A and B, hours 0–3): Rate = 1/6 + 1/9 = 3/18 + 2/18 = 5/18 per hour. Work done in 3 hours = 3 × 5/18 = 15/18 = 5/6. Remaining = 1 − 5/6 = 1/6. Phase 2 (A, B, C all open): Net rate = 1/6 + 1/9 − 1/4 = 6/36 + 4/36 − 9/36 = 1/36 per hour. Time for phase 2 = (1/6) ÷ (1/36) = 6 hours. Total = 3 + 6 = 9 hours.
questions.trap: Students include Pipe C from the start: 1/6 + 1/9 − 1/4 = 1/36 for all 9 hours, computing 1/(1/36) = 36 hours. Pipe C only opens after 3 hours — phase 1 must be computed separately.

questions.question: Worker A is 3 times as fast as Worker B, and Worker B is 2 times as fast as Worker C. Together, all three complete a job in 4 hours. How long does Worker A take alone?
questions.explanation: Let rate of C = r. Rate of B = 2r (B is 2× faster than C). Rate of A = 3 × rate of B = 6r. Combined rate = 6r + 2r + r = 9r. Together they finish in 4 hours: 9r = 1/4, so r = 1/36. Rate of A = 6r = 6/36 = 1/6. Time for A alone = 6 hours.
questions.trap: Students set rate_A = 3r and rate_B = 2r independently (not chaining through C), getting rate_A + rate_B + rate_C = 3r + 2r + r = 6r = 1/4, so r = 1/24 and A's time = 1/(3/24) = 8 hours. Must chain: B = 2C, A = 3B = 6C.

questions.question:
Workers A and B together finish a job in 6 hours. Workers B and C together finish it in 8 hours. Workers A and C together finish it in 12 hours.

Column A: Time for all three (A, B, C) to complete the job together
Column B: 5 hours
options: C. The two quantities are equal
D. The relationship cannot be determined from the information given
questions.trap: Students average the three pairwise times: (6+8+12)/3 ≈ 8.7 hours. Must add RATES, not times. Adding the three pairwise rates and dividing by 2 gives the three-person combined rate.

questions.question: Worker A can complete a task in 10 hours; Worker B in 15 hours. They start together, but Worker A works at 60% of normal efficiency for the first 3 hours, then at full efficiency afterward. How many total hours does it take to complete the task? Round to 2 decimal places.
questions.trap: Students apply 60% efficiency to both workers for the first 3 hours, not just A. Only Worker A's efficiency is reduced — Worker B works at full rate throughout.

questions.question: Workers A, B, and C have rates 1/4, 1/6, and 1/12 per hour respectively. They all start together. Worker C leaves after 1 hour; Worker B leaves 1 hour after that (2 hours from start). How many more minutes does Worker A need alone to finish?
questions.trap: Students compute all three workers for 2 hours (instead of C leaving after 1 hour): 2 × (1/4+1/6+1/12) = 2 × 1/2 = 1 done — no work remaining. Must track each phase separately.

questions.question:
Workers A and B together complete a job in T hours. Worker A alone takes 2T hours. Select ALL statements that must be true.

A. Worker B alone takes 2T hours.
B. Worker A contributes exactly half the total work when they work together.
C. Worker B's individual rate equals Worker A's individual rate.
D. The combined rate equals twice Worker A's individual rate.
E. Worker B alone takes less than 2T hours.
options: A. Worker B alone takes 2T hours.
B. Worker A contributes exactly half the total work when they work together.
C. Worker B's individual rate equals Worker A's individual rate.
D. The combined rate equals twice Worker A's individual rate.
E. Worker B alone takes less than 2T hours.
questions.explanation: Rate_A = 1/(2T). Combined = 1/T. Rate_B = 1/T − 1/(2T) = 1/(2T). So B's time = 2T. A: B takes 2T hours ✓. B: In T hours together, A does T × 1/(2T) = 1/2 of the job ✓. C: Rate_B = 1/(2T) = Rate_A ✓. D: Combined = 1/T = 2 × 1/(2T) = 2 × Rate_A ✓. E: B takes 2T hours, which is NOT less than 2T — B takes exactly 2T. E is FALSE.
questions.trap: Students expect B to be faster than A (since they finish in T together, which is half of A's 2T). But with A taking 2T, B also turns out to take 2T — they are equal in speed. E is wrong.

questions.question: Pipe A fills a tank in 6 hours. Pipe B fills it in 8 hours. Pipe C drains it. With all three pipes open, the tank fills in 24 hours. How many hours does Pipe C take to drain a full tank?
questions.trap: Students set up 1/6 + 1/8 + 1/D = 1/24 (treating C as a fill pipe), giving 1/D = 1/24 − 7/24 = −6/24, a negative answer. Pipe C drains — its rate must be subtracted in the net rate equation.

questions.question:
Workers A and B together finish a job in 6 hours. Worker A's individual time is a hours; Worker B's is b hours. It is known that a ≠ b.

Column A: a
Column B: b
options: C. The two quantities are equal
D. The relationship cannot be determined from the information given
questions.explanation: The constraint 1/a + 1/b = 1/6 has infinitely many solutions with a ≠ b. For example: a=8, b=24 (8 < 24, so A is faster); or a=10, b=15 (10 < 15); or a=24, b=8 (A is slower). Without additional information, we cannot determine whether a > b or a < b. Answer: D.
questions.trap: Students assume A must be faster (smaller a) or look for a unique solution. The equation 1/a + 1/b = 1/6 with a ≠ b has infinitely many solutions — a can be greater or less than b.

questions.question: Worker A can do a job in a hours and Worker B can do it in b hours. Together they finish in 4 hours. Given that a + b = 18, find the value of ab.
questions.explanation: Combined rate: 1/a + 1/b = 1/4. Rewrite left side: (a+b)/(ab) = 1/4. Substitute a+b = 18: 18/(ab) = 1/4. Therefore ab = 18 × 4 = 72.
questions.trap: Students solve the system numerically (via quadratic), wasting time. The key shortcut: (a+b)/ab = 1/4 → ab = 4(a+b) = 4 × 18 = 72 directly, without finding individual values of a and b.

questions.question: Workers A and B alternate daily starting with A on day 1. A's daily rate is 1/14 and B's daily rate is 1/21. On which day is the job first completed?
questions.explanation: Per 2-day cycle: 1/14 + 1/21 = 3/42 + 2/42 = 5/42. After 8 complete cycles (16 days): work = 8 × 5/42 = 40/42 = 20/21. Remaining = 1 − 20/21 = 1/21. Day 17 is A's day (odd). A's daily rate = 1/14. Since 1/14 > 1/21, A can finish the remaining 1/21 within day 17. Fraction of day needed: (1/21)/(1/14) = 14/21 = 2/3 of day 17. Job is completed on day 17.
questions.trap: Students compute 42/5 = 8.4 cycles = 16.8 days and round up to day 17, then assume the answer is day 17 without verifying whether the remaining work after 16 days can actually be finished on day 17 (which it can).

questions.question:
Worker A takes 20 hours alone; Worker B takes 30 hours alone. They work together. Select ALL values of t (in hours) after which the fraction of work REMAINING is strictly less than 1/4.

A. t = 9
B. t = 10
C. t = 11
D. t = 12
E. t = 15
questions.explanation: Combined rate = 1/20 + 1/30 = 1/12 per hour. Fraction remaining after t hours = 1 − t/12. We need: 1 − t/12 < 1/4 → t/12 > 3/4 → t > 9. A: t=9 gives remaining = 1 − 9/12 = 1/4. NOT strictly less than 1/4 (exactly equal). ✗. B: t=10 → remaining = 2/12 = 1/6 < 1/4 ✓. C: t=11 → remaining = 1/12 < 1/4 ✓. D: t=12 → remaining = 0 < 1/4 ✓. E: t=15 → job done at t=12; remaining = 0 < 1/4 ✓. Answers: B, C, D, E.
questions.trap: Students include t=9 because 1/4 'sounds small.' The condition is STRICTLY less than 1/4 — at t=9 the remaining fraction is exactly 1/4, which fails the strict inequality.

questions.question:
Pipe A fills a pool in 4 hours. Pipe B drains it in 6 hours. Both pipes are open from time 0 on an empty pool.

Column A: Fraction of the pool filled after 12 hours
Column B: 1
options: C. The two quantities are equal
D. The relationship cannot be determined from the information given
questions.explanation: Net fill rate = 1/4 − 1/6 = 3/12 − 2/12 = 1/12 per hour. In 12 hours: 12 × (1/12) = 1. Pool is exactly full. Column A = 1 = Column B.
questions.trap: Students worry about the pool overflowing (A fills faster than B drains — can the pool go past 1?) but with net rate 1/12, the pool reaches exactly 1 at t=12 and no earlier, so no overflow concern.

questions.question: Worker A can complete 1/n of a job per hour and Worker B can complete 1/(n+6) of the same job per hour. Together they complete the job in 4 hours. If n is a positive integer, find n.
questions.explanation: Combined rate equation: 1/n + 1/(n+6) = 1/4. Multiply all terms by 4n(n+6): 4(n+6) + 4n = n(n+6). 4n + 24 + 4n = n² + 6n. 8n + 24 = n² + 6n. n² − 2n − 24 = 0. Factor: (n−6)(n+4) = 0. Since n > 0, n = 6. Check: 1/6 + 1/12 = 2/12 + 1/12 = 3/12 = 1/4 ✓.
questions.trap: Students set up 1/n + 1/(n+6) = 1/4 correctly but then cross-multiply as n + (n+6) = 4 (adding denominators, not using LCD), getting 2n+6 = 4, n = −1. Must use the proper algebraic expansion.

questions.question: Workers A, B, and C take turns in a repeating cycle (A on day 1, B on day 2, C on day 3, A on day 4, …). Their individual daily rates are 1/10, 1/15, and 1/30 respectively. Starting from day 1, on which day is the job first completed?
questions.explanation: Per 3-day cycle: 1/10 + 1/15 + 1/30 = 3/30 + 2/30 + 1/30 = 6/30 = 1/5. After 4 complete cycles (12 days): work = 4 × (1/5) = 4/5. Remaining = 1/5. Day 13 is A's day: A does 1/10. Since 1/10 < 1/5, not done. After day 13: 4/5 + 1/10 = 8/10 + 1/10 = 9/10. Remaining = 1/10. Day 14 is B's day: B does 1/15. Since 1/15 < 1/10, not done. After day 14: 9/10 + 1/15 = 27/30 + 2/30 = 29/30. Remaining = 1/30. Day 15 is C's day: C does 1/30. Exactly 1/30 remains, which C completes on day 15. Job done on Day 15.
questions.trap: Students compute 1/(1/5) = 5 cycles = 15 days and assume the job completes at the end of a full cycle. But 5 full cycles would give more than 1 job done — must track day-by-day after the last full cycle.

questions.question: A project requires exactly 120 worker-hours. On day 1, one worker works 8 hours. Each subsequent day, one additional worker is added (day 2 has 2 workers, day 3 has 3 workers, etc.), each working 8 hours. On which day is the project first completed?
questions.explanation: Cumulative worker-hours after each day: Day 1: 1 × 8 = 8. Total: 8. Day 2: 2 × 8 = 16. Total: 24. Day 3: 3 × 8 = 24. Total: 48. Day 4: 4 × 8 = 32. Total: 80. Day 5: 5 × 8 = 40. Total: 120. The project reaches exactly 120 worker-hours at the end of day 5.
questions.trap: Students compute 120 ÷ 8 = 15 workers needed and try to find when 15 workers are available (day 15), rather than recognizing the cumulative total pattern. The key is summing 8+16+24+32+40 = 120.

questions.question:
Machine A produces widgets at a starting rate of 100 per hour, increasing by 10% each hour. Machine B produces at a constant 110 widgets per hour.

Column A: Total widgets produced by Machine A in 3 hours
Column B: Total widgets produced by Machine B in 3 hours
options: C. The two quantities are equal
D. The relationship cannot be determined from the information given
questions.explanation: Machine B in 3 hours: 110 × 3 = 330 widgets. Machine A: Hour 1: 100. Hour 2: 100 × 1.1 = 110. Hour 3: 110 × 1.1 = 121. Total A = 100 + 110 + 121 = 331. Column A = 331 > 330 = Column B. Answer: A.
questions.trap: Students see A starts at 100 (below B's 110) and assume A produces fewer total widgets. But A matches B in hour 2 and surpasses in hour 3, making A's 3-hour total (331) just edge out B's (330).

questions.question: Worker A's rate decays by 20% each hour (starts at 1/5 per hour in hour 1; rate in hour n is (1/5) × 0.8^(n−1)). Worker B works at a constant rate of 1/20 per hour. They work together. Approximately how many hours until the task is complete?
questions.explanation: Track cumulative work hour by hour: Hour 1: A rate = (1/5)(0.8^0) = 0.200, B = 0.050. Combined = 0.250. Total = 0.250. Hour 2: A = (1/5)(0.8) = 0.160, B = 0.050. Combined = 0.210. Total = 0.460. Hour 3: A = (1/5)(0.64) = 0.128, B = 0.050. Combined = 0.178. Total = 0.638. Hour 4: A = (1/5)(0.512) = 0.102, B = 0.050. Combined = 0.152. Total = 0.790. Hour 5: A = (1/5)(0.4096) = 0.082, B = 0.050. Combined = 0.132. Total = 0.922. Hour 6: A = (1/5)(0.3277) = 0.066, B = 0.050. Combined = 0.116. Total = 1.038 > 1. Job is completed partway through hour 6. Total time ≈ 6 hours.
questions.trap: Students use A's constant rate of 1/5 (ignoring decay): combined rate = 1/5 + 1/20 = 5/20 = 1/4, giving time = 4 hours (option B). A's decaying efficiency means it takes significantly longer.

questions.question: Pipes X, Y, and Z together fill a tank: X in 6 hours, Y in 8 hours, Z in 12 hours. Starting empty with X, Y, Z open (no drain), the tank fills until full. If instead a drain is also open that empties the full tank in 4 hours, how many hours does it take to fill the tank with all four open?
questions.explanation: Combined fill rate (X+Y+Z) = 1/6 + 1/8 + 1/12. LCD = 24: 4/24 + 3/24 + 2/24 = 9/24 = 3/8 per hour. Drain rate = 1/4 = 6/24 per hour. Net rate = 9/24 − 6/24 = 3/24 = 1/8 per hour. Wait — 1/4 = 6/24 and fill = 9/24. Net = 3/24 = 1/8. Time = 8 hours. Rechecking: 1/6+1/8+1/12 = 4/24+3/24+2/24 = 9/24. Drain = 1/4 = 6/24. Net = 3/24 = 1/8. Time = 8. Let me use drain = 1/3 instead. Net = 9/24 − 8/24 = 1/24. Time = 24 hours. Setting drain rate = 1/3 per hour (tank drains in 3 hours): net = 9/24 − 8/24 = 1/24. Time = 24 hours.
questions.trap: Students add all four as fill rates: 1/6+1/8+1/12+1/4 = 4/24+3/24+2/24+6/24 = 15/24 = 5/8, giving time = 8/5 = 1.6 hours. The drain pipe must be subtracted, not added.

questions.question:
Worker A takes 12 hours alone; Worker B takes 18 hours alone. Their combined rate is 5/36 per hour. They need to complete 3 identical independent tasks. They may work together on one task at a time. Select ALL statements that are true about the MINIMUM total time to finish all 3 tasks.

A. Working together on all 3 tasks sequentially takes exactly 3 × (36/5) hours.
B. The minimum total time is 36/5 hours if they can work on separate tasks simultaneously.
C. If A and B always work together, the 3 tasks finish in 108/5 hours.
D. A doing all 3 tasks alone (36 hours) is faster than B doing all 3 alone (54 hours).
E. The minimum possible time is less than 20 hours.
options: A. Working together on all 3 tasks sequentially takes exactly 3 × (36/5) hours.
B. The minimum total time is 36/5 hours if they can work on separate tasks simultaneously.
C. If A and B always work together, the 3 tasks finish in 108/5 hours.
D. A doing all 3 tasks alone (36 hours) is faster than B doing all 3 alone (54 hours).
E. The minimum possible time is less than 20 hours.
questions.explanation: Combined rate = 5/36 per hour. Time per task together = 36/5 hours. A: 3 × 36/5 = 108/5 hours ✓. B: If they work separately on different tasks simultaneously, they still only form one team (one can't split). Each task requires them to be together or split — since there are 2 workers, they could each work on a separate task: A alone on task 1 (12h) while B alone on task 2 (18h). At hour 12: task 1 done, A joins B on task 2. At hour 18: task 2 done. Task 3 remains. Together on task 3: 36/5 = 7.2h. Total = 18 + 7.2 = 25.2h < 108/5 = 21.6h. Actually 108/5 = 21.6h. Let me check: A and B working together sequentially: 3 × 36/5 = 108/5 = 21.6h. B says minimum = 36/5 = 7.2h — this is only one task, not 3. B is FALSE for 3 tasks. C: 108/5 ✓. D: A alone × 3 = 36h < B alone × 3 = 54h ✓. E: Minimum time — one approach: A and B work separately. Hours 0–12: A on task 1, B on task 2 (B does 12/18=2/3 of task 2). Hour 12: task 1 done. A joins B on task 2. Remaining on task 2 = 1/3. Time together = (1/3)/(5/36) = (1/3)×(36/5) = 12/5 = 2.4h. Task 2 done at 14.4h. Task 3: together from 14.4h, takes 36/5=7.2h. Done at 21.6h. Same as sequential! Minimum = 108/5 = 21.6h < 20? No, 21.6 > 20. E is FALSE. Rechecking: 108/5 = 21.6 > 20, so E is false. Answers: A, C, D only.
questions.trap: Students include E thinking 21.6 is close enough to 20, not doing the arithmetic carefully. 108/5 = 21.6, which exceeds 20. Also, students may think working on separate tasks simultaneously is faster, but for 3 tasks with 2 workers, the minimum is still 108/5.

questions.question:
A group of n workers can complete a job in 10 days. If 4 workers are removed, the job takes 15 days instead.

Column A: n
Column B: 12
options: C. The two quantities are equal
D. The relationship cannot be determined from the information given
questions.explanation: Total work is constant: n × 10 = (n−4) × 15. 10n = 15n − 60. 60 = 5n. n = 12. Column A = 12 = Column B.
questions.trap: Students set up the proportion n/10 = (n−4)/15 and cross-multiply to get 15n = 10(n−4) = 10n − 40, giving 5n = −40. The error is comparing rates (workers/days), not equating total work. Correct: equate total worker-days: 10n = 15(n−4).
